name: Incremental Rates of Profit in US Manufacturing, 1960–1989

book_quotes.quote: But the picture changes substantially when we consider the profit rates on new investment, that is, the incremental rate of return on capital (figure 2.13). This is measured here as the change in gross profits divided by the gross investment in the previous year (Christodoulopoulos 1995, 138–140; Shaikh 1998b, 395).

Figure 2.13 Incremental Rates of Profit in US Manufacturing, 1960–1989. Discussed in chapter 7 in relation to figure 7.14. Appendix 7.2 Data Tables for Chapter 7 (available online at http://www.anwarshaikhecon.org/)

primary_source.agency: Constructed (Shaikh / Christodoulopoulos) from OECD / BEA industry-level data
primary_source.table_or_series_id: Incremental profit rate r* = ΔPG / IG(–1) by manufacturing sub-sector

formula: r* = PG / IG(-1) where PG = gross profits, IG = gross investment in previous year (per footnote 6, p. 67)

components.label: Gross investment (IG) lagged one year by sector

extension_candidates.subseries_label: AMECO Marginal Efficiency of Capital (MEC) — analogous concept
extension_candidates.source: European Commission DG ECFIN AMECO Database
concerns: Per Shaikh's own footnote 6 on p. 68, AMECO MEC follows essentially the same procedure (Δgross output / lagged investment). Concept-similar successor but defined on output rather than profits — not a direct equivalent.

methodology_notes: Formula explicitly given in footnote 6 (book p. 68): r* = PG / IG(-1). Both numerator and denominator invariant to CCAdj (capital consumption adjustment) and to estimates of capital stock or useful life.
Shaikh prefers this measure for arbitrage analysis because incremental rates 'cross over' frequently across sectors — 'profit rate equalization in its true form'.
open_questions: AMECO MEC uses gross output instead of profits in the numerator; if used for extension, document this concept difference per anu-framework no-proxy rule.
